crime: One afternoon the girls played hide-and-seek across the courtyard and the flower field. She took the best hiding spot and stayed, hands folded. The game had been called an hour earlier and moved inside; no one noticed her missing.
When she walked in at the lock bell, the seeker girl looked up: oh — were you playing?

The next morning, at the end of free time, she prepared the library's far aisle: set a shelf-board against the tall stack to take its lean, pried the wedge-blocks from its base and pocketed them, and ran a cord from the board to the aisle's blind end.

The ten-o'clock cell-stay sealed the room. Lunch kept it empty; the girls ate at the long tables below. She let the meal begin, then settled at the blind end behind the stack and waited for the seeker girl.
Mid-meal the seeker girl set down her spoon and walked out — up the staircase, past two girls who greeted her and got no answer. No one could later find the errand.

The seeker girl came up the far aisle as if remembering something there. From the blind end she pulled the cord; the stack toppled across the aisle. She was through the door and down the staircase when the crash brought the girls up from below.
The seeker girl beneath the toppled stack, buried in books. Where the wedge-blocks had sat: clean dust-shadows and fresh pry marks.

A wordless walk out of a half-eaten meal, past greetings, to an aisle with no errand at its end — retraced and found hollow. A drawn walk ends where the waiter waits; one girl had been missing from the tables the whole meal and joined the crowd only from the staircase, after the crash.
fields.epitaph: She waited her whole life to be found.
fields.epithet: She Who Kept Her Hiding Place
execution: Rows of iron screens rose on the execution stand, making lanes.
She was set loose among them, unbound. A single iron figure began walking the lanes from the far end, turning always toward where she stood.
She ran; it followed. She hid behind a screen; it slowed, then turned the corner. Hiding bought minutes. Stillness bought nothing.
It found her, and its iron arms closed around her against its frame and did not open.

magic.name: the Homing Wait
magic.text: The Homing Wait — When she settles and waits for one person, that person's feet set out toward her at once and bring them to her before the day is out, by a way that seems their own idea; she must keep her place while the waiting runs. Being drawn is not being sought.
